evidence_quotes.quote: the express intent of making them online-only, even for single-player experiences.
the constant implementation of online requirements in order to play games.
to have them deny letting players legally purchase games on platforms like Steam and Itch.io,

paragraphs.text: To whomever it may concern, I am writing this feedback form to formally request that you look towards including provisions regarding the ownership of digital assets, I.e. videogames. Recently, there have been numerous claims from videogame companies and distributors of the ideas that "Players should get comfortable not owning their games" and "Players will own nothing and be happy about it." This, frankly, is unacceptable as many gamers who purchase games digitally now, especially with the decline in retail stores to purchase games, will be at risk of losing access to their games that they paid full price for. One of the big problems is the constant implementation of online requirements in order to play games. Companies are now often creating games with the express intent of making them online-only, even for single-player experiences. This helps provide them the opportunity to prevent any external modification to the game the player has purchased, such as preventing them from being able to implement disability mods not included in the base game i.e. a keyboard layout customiser that allows a person to play the game with one hand on the left hand side of the keyboard might not be included in the game, restricting the players access to the game. This also allows them to revoke the game at any time should the user not play it for a few years, meaning that when/if the player inevitably wants to play the game again, it might not be there anymore, with no refund made available. Very recently, an organisation by the name of "Collective Shout" has also been looking to remove games from digital libraries. They have been pressuring payment platforms like VISA and Mastercard to have them deny letting players legally purchase games on platforms like Steam and Itch.io, particularly targeting games of a pornographic nature initially. Their reasoning for this in particular is to "protect children from graphic content online." This, I believe, is a lie. My reasoning for my suspicion is that they have also seemingly targeted other games that had no relation to the ones being targeted. In essence, it was about controlling what people could and couldn't buy. This is bad behaviour and should be discouraged as it is taking away people's freedoms under the guise of "protecting children." By removing games, they are taking away digital freedoms from people. So I urge you to consider adding a section aimed at protecting rights to digital assets like videogames to this act. Protecting our rights to the digital things that we purchase is important because it makes sure that companies cannot force us to "own nothing and be happy about it." When we purchase an item like a videogame, where we don't expect a physical copy of it but we do expect to be able to use it whenever we want, we should and rightfully do expect to now own that item. If a company or organisation can take away that item from us, then we should be allowed to pirate their game as we apparently "don't own" our game, unless they want to admit to stealing from us, the players. Thank you for your consideration.
